nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the island of Borneo, where it grows as a rheophyte on rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams; like other Bucephalandra it is periodically emersed and resubmerged as stream water levels rise and fall

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape for attaching the rhizome — never bury the rhizome in substrate
moderate water flow across the rhizome to mimic Borneo rheophyte stream conditions
low to medium lighting; moderate illumination brings out the bluish-purple iridescence on the leaves
stable, biologically mature tank — Bucephalandra is generally reported to do poorly in newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra catherinae is a true (formally described) species in the Borneo-endemic rheophyte genus Bucephalandra, treated in the hobby much like Anubias. It is a small, creeping form with narrow, slightly wavy deep-green leaves that can take on bluish-purple iridescence under moderate light; the rhizome must be attached to hardscape and never buried.
careNotes: Bucephalandra catherinae is one of the formally described species in the Borneo-endemic genus Bucephalandra (family Araceae), as opposed to the many trade cultivars sold under invented names. Husbandry is essentially identical to the genus as a whole (see the parent Bucephalandra entry, fw-plant-016): the creeping rhizome is attached to lava rock, driftwood or slate using super glue gel, sewing thread or fishing line and left exposed to the water column — burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause it to rot. Modern Aquarium and Aquarium Co-Op both report a tolerated temperature range of roughly 21-28 °C (70-82 °F); reported pH ranges cluster around 6.0-7.5 across hobby sources, with Aquarium Co-Op giving a wider 6-8 envelope. Lighting needs are flexible: low light is sufficient for slow growth, while medium light brings out the species' characteristic bluish-purple iridescence (a structural-color effect from leaf microstructure rather than pigment). CO2 is generally considered optional but produces visibly better growth and color, particularly for this species per Modern Aquarium. The plant absorbs nutrients from the water column, so substrate type is largely irrelevant and root tabs are not needed; light liquid fertilization is sufficient. Growth is very slow — commonly reported as one new leaf every 2-4 weeks in low-tech setups. Catherinae is a small, creeping form with narrow rippled leaves: hobby sources describe it as short and low-growing, in the roughly 5-10 cm size class typical of creeping Bucephalandra in Aquarium Co-Op's data. Several trade variants are sold under this species name, including 'Catherinae Dark Carpet', 'Catherinae Green', 'Catherinae Red' and 'Super Mini Catherinae'; husbandry across these variants is the same. Like other Bucephalandra, freshly imported plants that were grown emersed can shed their original leaves when first submerged ('buce melt'); as long as the rhizome stays firm and white/green inside, recovery from the rhizome is generally reported. Best introduced into an established, stable tank rather than a newly cycled one.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: once the plant has established and put out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe/spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirms Borneo stream origin, temperature 70-82°F, low-to-moderate lighting with moderate light enhancing coloration, CO2 recommended for optimal growth/color, slow growth, foreground-to-midground epiphytic placement, attachment to rocks/driftwood (not substrate), rhizome-division propagation, undemanding (beginner-intermediate) care, and bluish-purple coloration on rippled green leaves.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Secondary (used because the designated Buce Plant secondary returned HTTP 404 on a single fetch attempt and was abandoned per playbook policy). Genus-level care guide; cross-checked temperature 70-82°F (21-28°C), pH 6-8, creeping-species size class 2-4 inches / 5-10 cm, low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, slow growth, epiphytic foreground/midground placement, no substrate required, all-in-one liquid fertilization, propagation by rhizome cutting. Does not have a species-specific page for B. catherinae, so genus-level data was applied.
